title: Why This Setup Is Unusual
concept: Every other model in the curriculum minimizes one shared loss in one direction; a GAN optimizes two competing losses where one network's progress can worsen the other's — setting up the need for a formal minimax objective.

exercises.prompt: Recap: for a fake image scored p = 0.4, is Loss_D or Loss_G larger?

options.text: Loss_G is larger (≈0.92 vs ≈0.51) — the generator hasn't fooled the discriminator well yet

exercises.explain: Correct! At p=0.4 the fake was mostly caught, so the generator (not the discriminator) pays the bigger loss.

cards.title: Every other lesson had one arrow
cards.body: Look back across everything you've trained so far — regression models, classifiers, segmentation networks, even the CV capstone pipeline. Every single one had one number to shrink and one direction to walk: downhill. GANs break that pattern completely.

cards.title: One-Directional vs. Adversarial Training
cards.body: In ordinary supervised training, one loss function measures "wrongness," and every update step makes that number smaller for the whole model. In a GAN, there are two loss functions with opposite goals: the discriminator wants to tell real from fake, and the generator wants to make that impossible. One network's progress can make the other's loss WORSE.

cards.title: A one-way ski slope vs. a seesaw
cards.body: Normal training is like skiing downhill — gravity (gradient descent) always pulls you toward a single valley floor. GAN training is like a seesaw with two riders: pushing your side down necessarily pushes the other side up. There's no shared "bottom" both networks glide toward together.

cards.title: Recalling this unit's arc
cards.body: You've now seen the discriminator's Binary Cross-Entropy loss (Unit 67's formula, reused), the generator's fool-the-judge loss, the alternating-update training loop, and a worked numeric example where one score produced two very different loss values. Nowhere else in the curriculum has one score fed two competing loss functions at once.

cards.title: It's not just 'harder,' it's structurally different
cards.body: You might think GAN training is simply a trickier version of the same one-loss recipe used everywhere else. In reality, it's a fundamentally different setup: a genuine two-player game, not a single optimization problem. That's *why* GAN training can oscillate or destabilize in ways a normal classifier never does.

cards.title: Every Prior Model vs. GAN Training
cards.body: Comparing the shape of the optimization problem:

left.text:
One loss function
One direction: always down
Convergence means "loss near zero"

right.text:
Two competing loss functions
One network's "down" is the other's "up"
Convergence means an ongoing balance, not zero loss

exercises.prompt: What makes GAN training structurally different from every other model trained earlier in this curriculum?

options.text: It optimizes two separate, opposing loss functions instead of one shared loss that everyone improves together
It doesn't use gradients at all
It only ever trains for a single step total

exercises.explain: Correct — two competing losses instead of one cooperative loss is the structural difference.

exercises.prompt: True or false: in a well-trained GAN, both Loss_D and Loss_G should ideally shrink all the way to zero, just like in ordinary supervised training.
exercises.explain: False. Loss_D near zero would mean the generator is failing completely, and vice versa — the goal is an ongoing balance, not both hitting zero.

exercises.sentence: Unlike a normal classifier where every update makes the model's single loss go down, in a GAN one network's improvement can make the other network's loss go ___.
exercises.explain: The two losses are adversarial — one network's gain is routinely the other's pain.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best captures why GAN training is considered unusual compared to the rest of this curriculum?

options.text: Two networks optimize opposing loss functions in alternating turns, rather than one network minimizing one shared loss
GANs don't require any labels at all, unlike everything before them
GANs are the only models in the curriculum that use neural networks

exercises.explain: Correct — the opposing, alternating two-loss structure is what sets GAN training apart.

exercises.prompt: You now understand both individual losses (Loss_D and Loss_G) and know they pull in opposite directions. What's the natural next question to ask about GAN training?

options.text: Is there a single mathematical objective that formally captures both networks' opposing goals in one expression — the minimax objective?
How do we eliminate the generator's loss entirely?
How do we merge both networks into a single classifier?
How do we make the discriminator ignore fake images?

exercises.explain: Exactly! Both losses are really two views of one game. Next unit: the full minimax training objective that formally ties Loss_D and Loss_G together into a single equation.
